name: Data Exfiltration via DNS Tunnelling
severity: High to Critical (especially if sensitive data is confirmed to be
scenario: An attacker uses DNS as a communication channel to exfiltrate data or maintain command and control. DNS tunnelling disguises malicious payloads or stolen data inside DNS queries, bypassing traditional detection since DNS traffic is usually allowed.
tools: DNS Logging Platforms (e.g., Infoblox, Bind logs, Windows DNS logs); NDR (e.g., Corelight/Zeek, Darktrace, ExtraHop); SIEM (e.g., Splunk, Sentinel, QRadar); EDR (e.g., CrowdStrike, Cortex XDR); Threat Intelligence (e.g., Recorded Future, MISP, AbuseIPDB); Firewall and proxy logs

detSteps.title: Detect high-entropy subdomain queries indicating encoded data
detSteps.detail: DNS tunnelling encodes data as base32/base64 in subdomain labels — these appear as long, alphanumeric, semantically meaningless strings. Look for query lengths over 50 characters, high query volume to a single domain, and abuse of TXT or NULL record types.

queries.splunk: index=network sourcetype=dns | stats count avg(len(query)) as avg_qlen values(record_type) as rtypes by src_ip, query | where avg_qlen > 50 OR count > 300 | sort -count
queries.velociraptor: SELECT Laddr, Lport, Raddr, Rport, State, Pid, Name, Ctime FROM netstat() WHERE Rport IN (22,53,80,135,139,443,445,3389,8080,8443) ORDER BY Ctime DESC LIMIT 200
queries.carbon_black: netconn_ipaddr:* AND netconn_port:* | stats count by process_name, netconn_ipaddr, netconn_port, device_name

detSteps.title: Analyse subdomain entropy and pattern regularity
detSteps.detail: Legitimate traffic has readable subdomain labels. Tunnel tools produce fixed-length, alphanumeric-only labels that repeat at regular intervals (beaconing). The regularity of query timing is itself an indicator.

queries.splunk: index=network sourcetype=dns | rex field=query "^(?<sub>[^.]+)\\." | eval sub_len=len(sub) | stats count avg(sub_len) as avg_len by query | where avg_len > 30 | sort -avg_len
queries.velociraptor: SELECT Laddr, Lport, Raddr, Rport, State, Pid, Name, Ctime FROM netstat() WHERE Rport IN (22,53,80,135,139,443,445,3389,8080,8443) ORDER BY Ctime DESC LIMIT 200
queries.carbon_black: netconn_ipaddr:* AND netconn_port:* | stats count by process_name, netconn_ipaddr, netconn_port, device_name

detSteps.title: Correlate DNS activity with outbound network flow
detSteps.detail: After DNS resolution, tunnelling tools often fall back to direct TCP/UDP connections to the same destination on non-standard ports. Look for connections from the same host to the resolved IP on unusual ports shortly after the DNS queries.

queries.splunk: index=network (sourcetype=firewall OR sourcetype=netflow) src_ip=[suspect_ip] | stats count by dest_ip, dest_port | where dest_port NOT IN (80,443,53,22) | sort -count
queries.velociraptor: SELECT Laddr, Lport, Raddr, Rport, State, Pid, Name, Ctime FROM netstat() WHERE Rport IN (22,53,80,135,139,443,445,3389,8080,8443) ORDER BY Ctime DESC LIMIT 200
queries.carbon_black: netconn_ipaddr:* AND netconn_port:* | stats count by process_name, netconn_ipaddr, netconn_port, device_name

detSteps.title: Determine scope — other hosts using the same tunnel domain
detSteps.detail: Find all internal hosts that have queried the suspect tunnelling domain. Any host that received a successful response is a potential infection candidate.

queries.splunk: index=network sourcetype=dns query="*[suspect_domain]*" | stats dc(src_ip) as hosts values(src_ip) as host_list
queries.velociraptor: SELECT Laddr, Lport, Raddr, Rport, State, Pid, Name, Ctime FROM netstat() WHERE Rport IN (22,53,80,135,139,443,445,3389,8080,8443) ORDER BY Ctime DESC LIMIT 200
queries.carbon_black: netconn_ipaddr:* AND netconn_port:* | stats count by process_name, netconn_ipaddr, netconn_port, device_name

contSteps.title: Block the tunnel domain at the DNS resolver and firewall
contSteps.detail: Add the tunnelling domain to your DNS blocklist (return NXDOMAIN) and apply an outbound firewall rule blocking all traffic to the resolved IPs. Verify the block is in place and monitor for the same host attempting to use alternative C2 domains.

queries.splunk: index=network sourcetype=dns query="*[suspect_domain]*" earliest=-15m | stats count by src_ip
queries.velociraptor: SELECT Laddr, Lport, Raddr, Rport, State, Pid, Name, Ctime FROM netstat() WHERE Rport IN (22,53,80,135,139,443,445,3389,8080,8443) ORDER BY Ctime DESC LIMIT 200
queries.carbon_black: netconn_ipaddr:* AND netconn_port:* | stats count by process_name, netconn_ipaddr, netconn_port, device_name

contSteps.title: Isolate the originating host from the network
contSteps.detail: Network-isolate the host generating the tunnel traffic. If host access is limited, apply firewall rules to block all non-essential outbound traffic from that IP while the investigation continues.

eradSteps.title: Remove tunnelling tools or malware: From endpoints using EDR or forensic analysis
Patch exploited vulnerabilities: If attacker gained access through known weaknesses
Clean persistence mechanisms: Check for scheduled tasks, registry changes or startup scripts
Review DNS configurations: Ensure no external DNS bypasses exist on endpoints or servers

recSteps.title: Restore network connectivity: Once system is verified to be clean and containment controls are in place
Resume DNS services: Enforce forwarding through secure DNS infrastructure with inspection
Revalidate affected systems: Perform full scan and traffic monitoring on previously infected hosts
Update threat detection rules: Enhance SIEM, NDR and firewall rules with new indicators and patterns

metrics.name: DNS Logging Coverage
metrics.target: 100% of egress DNS activity logged and monitored
